hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: What Would You Do? -- Second Conditional
lesson.menu_desc: Ana has limited time before a release and must decide what to test. She weighs trade-offs and thinks in hypotheticals -- what she would do if she had more time, and how she would mitigate the risk -- using the second conditional (If I had more time, I would automate the checkout tests) -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 12 -- What Would You Do?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 12 -- What Would You Do?

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a balance between two good things you cannot both fully have
imagined, not real; used to think about a possible situation
a plan for something that might go wrong
to make a risk or a problem less serious
the negative part or disadvantage of a choice
to move something to a later time
to judge how good, bad or risky something is
giving good value for the time or money spent

paras: The release was set for Friday, and Ana had only two days to test a big new feature. She could not test everything, so she had to make smart decisions. She started by asking herself a hypothetical question: 'If I had more time, what would I automate first?' The answer was clear: the checkout flow, because it carries the money. So she made a trade-off. If she tested the checkout by hand every time, she would be safe but slow. If she wrote automated tests for it, she would spend a day now but save many days later. Automated tests were more cost-effective, so she chose them.

Next, she had to assess the risk of the parts she would not test deeply. The reporting screen was low risk: if it broke, users would still be able to pay, and the impact would be small. So she decided to postpone the deep testing of that screen to the next sprint. She could justify that choice to her team in one line: if the reporting screen failed, nobody would lose money, so it was a reasonable thing to leave for later. The downside was that a small visual bug might slip through, but that was a risk she was willing to accept.

Finally, Ana built a contingency plan, because a good tester always asks 'what if'. If the automated tests found a serious problem on Friday morning, the team would postpone the release by a few hours rather than ship a broken checkout. To mitigate the risk even more, she kept the old version ready: if the new feature failed badly, they would roll back and nobody would notice. When her manager asked her to explain the plan, she did not sound nervous. She had thought through every hypothetical, so she could answer any 'what would you do if...' with a calm, reasonable answer.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 reading, risk-based test planning and decision-making

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Ana tests absolutely everything by hand because she does not trust automated tests.
With little time, Ana makes smart decisions: she weighs trade-offs, assesses risk and plans for 'what if', thinking in hypotheticals.
The text explains how to design a checkout page from zero.

items: Ana decided to automate the checkout tests because they were more cost-effective than testing by hand every time.
The text says automated tests would save many days later and were 'more cost-effective, so she chose them'.
Ana tested the reporting screen deeply because it was the highest-risk part of the feature.
The opposite: the reporting screen was low risk, so she decided 'to postpone the deep testing of that screen to the next sprint'.
Ana had a contingency plan: if the new feature failed badly, the team would roll back to the old version.
The text says she kept the old version ready: 'if the new feature failed badly, they would roll back and nobody would notice'.
When her manager asked about the plan, Ana sounded nervous and could not answer the 'what if' questions.
The text says she 'did not sound nervous' and 'could answer any what would you do if... with a calm, reasonable answer'.

option: automated tests. She decided to
the low-risk screen to the next sprint, and built a
the risk: if the feature failed, they would roll back.

gapfill.text: Notice the second conditional for hypothetical planning: If I HAD more time, I WOULD automate first. If the screen FAILED, nobody WOULD lose money. Form: If + past simple, would + base verb. Use it for imagined situations, not real facts.

bank.label: Useful sentences for talking about test decisions
items: If I had more time, I would automate the checkout tests first.
If we tested everything by hand, we would miss the deadline.
It is a trade-off between speed and safety.
If the reporting screen failed, nobody would lose money.
We could postpone the low-risk tests to the next sprint.
To mitigate the risk, we would keep the old version ready.
If the feature failed badly, we would roll back and ship later.
That is a reasonable risk, and I can justify it to the team.

lf.title: Plan it in the second conditional
items: more time, I would automate the checkout first. (imagined situation)
If we tested everything by hand, we
the old version ready. (what we would do)

lf.text: Second conditional = If + past simple, would + base verb. The past form ('had', 'failed') does not mean the past here; it marks the situation as imagined, not real. Use it to plan and to weigh options: 'If it broke, we would roll back.'

items.situation: You have only two days before a release. Say what you would test first, in the second conditional.
tips: If I had two days, I would automate the checkout tests first.
If I could only test one thing, I would test the payment flow.

items.situation: A screen is low risk. Justify postponing it, using a hypothetical.
tips: If the reporting screen failed, nobody would lose money, so I would leave it for the next sprint.
If that screen broke, the impact would be small, so it is a reasonable thing to postpone.

items.situation: Describe your contingency plan for Friday. What would you do if the tests found a serious bug?
tips: If the tests found a serious bug, we would postpone the release by a few hours.
If the feature failed badly, we would roll back to the old version.

items.situation: Explain a trade-off you would make between speed and safety.
tips: If we tested everything by hand, we would be safe but too slow.
It is a trade-off: if we automated it, we would spend a day now but save many days later.

items: You have limited time before a release. Say what you would test first and why, using the second conditional. Speak from your notes.
Now explain one trade-off you would make and one screen you would postpone. Justify it: if it failed, what would happen?
Give your whole plan out loud, with no notes: what you would automate, what you would postpone, and your contingency if the tests failed on Friday.
Your manager asks, 'What would you do if the automated tests failed one hour before the release?' Answer clearly and calmly, in the second conditional.

answerkey.title: Reveal the decision-making toolkit
list: Vocabulary: trade-off, hypothetical, contingency, to mitigate, downside, to postpone, to justify, to assess, alternative, reasonable, to reconsider, cost-effective.
Hypothetical question: 'If I had more time, what would I automate first?'
Weigh the trade-off: 'If we tested by hand, we would be safe but slow.'
Justify a decision: 'If the screen failed, nobody would lose money, so it is reasonable to postpone it.'
Contingency: 'If the feature failed badly, we would roll back.' Structure: assess the risk, choose the cost-effective option, plan for what if.
answerkey.note: A good tester thinks in hypotheticals before the release, not after. Weigh, decide, and plan for what if.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, this is David from the product side. I saw your test plan for Friday, and I want to talk through one thing before we lock it. You decided to automate the checkout and postpone the reporting screen, and I agree with that. But leadership is nervous, so let me ask you a hypothetical. If the automated tests found a serious bug on Friday morning, what would you do? Would you still ship, or would you hold the release? And a second one: if we had one more day, would you change anything in the plan, or would you keep it exactly as it is? I am not testing you, I just want to be able to answer these questions when leadership asks me. Think it through and send me two short lines. Thanks, Ana, the plan looks solid.

Hi Ana, it is Priya, your team lead. I want to give you a quick tip on planning under pressure, because you are getting really good at it. The secret is to think in hypotheticals before the release, not after. Ask yourself the 'what if' questions early. If this part broke, who would lose money? If I only had one day, what would I test first? When you answer those in your head, the trade-offs become obvious, and you can justify every decision to the team. And always build a contingency. Never say 'it will be fine'. Say 'if it failed, we would roll back'. That one sentence turns fear into a plan. One more thing: a trade-off is not a mistake. If you postpone a low-risk screen to save time for the checkout, that is not being lazy, that is being smart. Weigh it, decide it, and move on with confidence.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana planned her testing under pressure. First, she asked herself a hypothetical: if she had more time, what would she automate first? Then she made a trade-off and chose automated tests for the checkout, because they were more cost-effective. After that, she assessed the risk of the other screens and decided to postpone the low-risk one. Next, she built a contingency plan in case the tests failed on Friday. Finally, she kept the old version ready, so if the feature failed badly, they would roll back.
